rationale: The homes at this Humble, TX address display classic hip/pyramid roof forms common to Houston-area suburban construction. Shadow lengths cast from the eave edges onto the surrounding lawn measure approximately 20–30% of building width, consistent with a moderate 6:12 pitch rather than the shorter shadows of a 4:12 or the longer shadows of an 8:12. The ridge peaks are visibly elevated above the eave plane with well-defined slope faces, and the ridge-to-eave geometry shows the ridge sitting noticeably inward but not dramatically compressed — both hallmarks of a 6:12 slope. Regional norms for early-2000s Texas suburban tract housing strongly support 6:12 as the dominant pitch, and no steeply protruding dormers or unusually compressed roof planes are present to suggest deviation from this standard.
